SEED-AML-section_22_suspicious_transacti.raw_text: Banks shall monitor customer transactions for unusual patterns or sudden spikes in volume. Any suspicious transaction must be reported to the Financial Intelligence Unit - India (FIU-IND) within 7 days of arriving at a conclusion that the transaction is indeed suspicious or relates to proceeds of crime.

SEED-AML-section_24_cash_transaction_rep.raw_text: All cash transactions exceeding INR 10 Lakh or its equivalent in foreign currency, or a series of integrally connected cash transactions that aggregate to more than INR 10 Lakh in a single calendar month, must be reported to FIU-IND via Cash Transaction Reports (CTR) by the 15th of the succeeding month.

SEED-AML-section_30_watchlist_and_sancti.raw_text: Banks must run real-time sanctions screening of all customer names, beneficial owners, and cross-border transaction counterparties against the consolidated United Nations Security Council (UNSC) sanctions list, OFAC list, and local watchlists before onboarding and processing transactions.

SEED-AML-section_35_transaction_monitori.raw_text: All transaction alerts generated by the automated AML monitoring system must be investigated and closed within 30 days of alert generation. Alerts flagged as high-risk require immediate escalation to the Principal Officer for further investigation.

SEED-FOREX-section_10_reporting_of_liber.section_title: Section 10 - Reporting of Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS)
SEED-FOREX-section_10_reporting_of_liber.raw_text: All remittances made under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS) by resident individuals exceeding USD 250,000 per financial year require prior approval from the Reserve Bank of India. Transaction details and remittances must be reported in Form LRS on a monthly basis by the 10th of the succeeding month.

SEED-FOREX-section_16_foreign_travel_car.raw_text: Authorised Dealers may issue foreign travel cards to resident individuals traveling abroad up to the limit of USD 250,000 or equivalent per financial year. Loading or reloading of travel cards beyond this limit must be blocked automatically by the card issuance system.

SEED-FOREX-section_18_external_commercia.raw_text: Any External Commercial Borrowings (ECB) raised by eligible Indian entities must be reported to the Reserve Bank of India in Form ECB within 7 days from the end of the month in which the loan agreement is signed to obtain an LRN (Loan Registration Number).

SEED-FOREX-section_25_export_proceeds_re.raw_text: The realization and repatriation of export proceeds to India must be completed within 9 months from the date of export. For status holder exporters, the period of realization is 12 months from the date of export, unless extended by the Reserve Bank of India.

SEED-KYC-paragraph_38_customer_due_dilig.section_title: Paragraph 38 - Customer Due Diligence for Individuals
SEED-KYC-paragraph_38_customer_due_dilig.raw_text: Banks shall carry out Customer Due Diligence by obtaining the following documents from individual customers at the time of account opening. A certified copy of any one document notified by the Central Government as proof of identity along with one document as proof of address is mandatory. Permanent Account Number or Form 60 as defined in Income Tax Rules shall be obtained compulsorily. Aadhaar number shall be obtained from customers who voluntarily provide it. No account shall be opened in anonymous or fictitious names. The identity verification must be completed within 24 hours of account opening initiation.

SEED-KYC-paragraph_45_periodic_updation_.raw_text: Regulated entities shall ensure periodic updation of KYC records for all customers. The frequency of updation shall be at least once in two years for high risk customers, at least once in eight years for medium risk customers, and at least once in ten years for low risk customers. Banks shall obtain fresh KYC documents from customers when their risk profile changes from lower to higher category. Failure to complete periodic KYC updation shall result in partial freezing of the account whereby credits shall not be permitted. Only debit transactions essential for the livelihood of the customer shall be allowed after partial freeze. A notice period of thirty days shall be given to the customer before imposing partial freeze.

SEED-KYC-paragraph_56_video_customer_ide.raw_text: The Video Customer Identification Process may be carried out by a bank official using live video. The process shall include a live photograph of the customer, capture of the PAN card, and an OTP sent to the customer's mobile number registered with Aadhaar. The bank official conducting the V-CIP must be a trained employee. All V-CIP sessions must be recorded and stored for a minimum period of six months. The video quality must be sufficient to identify the customer clearly. Customers whose Aadhaar is not seeded with a mobile number shall not be eligible for V-CIP.

SEED-KYC-paragraph_65_central_kyc_record.raw_text: Banks shall register customers on the Central KYC Records Registry for all accounts opened on or after January 1, 2017. A unique fourteen digit KYC Identifier shall be issued to each customer registered on the CKYC Registry. Banks downloading KYC records of existing customers from the CKYC Registry shall carry out fresh physical verification of identity and address documents at the time of download. Physical re-verification of original documents is mandatory regardless of the availability of digital records in the Registry. No exemption from physical re-verification shall be granted to any category of customer including those with V-CIP history.

SEED-KYC-paragraph_72_enhanced_due_dilig.section_title: Paragraph 72 - Enhanced Due Diligence for High Risk Customers
SEED-KYC-paragraph_72_enhanced_due_dilig.raw_text: Banks shall apply enhanced due diligence measures for all customers classified as high risk. High risk customers include Politically Exposed Persons and their family members and close associates. Enhanced due diligence shall include obtaining source of funds documentation, senior management approval for account opening, and enhanced ongoing monitoring at quarterly intervals. Banks shall not open accounts for foreign nationals from high risk jurisdictions without prior approval from the Compliance department head. All high risk account opening decisions must be documented with specific reasons recorded.

SEED-KYC-paragraph_89_record_keeping_and.section_title: Paragraph 89 - Record Keeping and Reporting Obligations
SEED-KYC-paragraph_89_record_keeping_and.raw_text: All records relating to the identity of customers and their transactions shall be maintained for a minimum period of five years after the business relationship has ended. Transaction records exceeding the prescribed threshold of ten lakh rupees shall be reported to the Financial Intelligence Unit within seven working days. Suspicious transaction reports shall be filed within seven working days of the transaction being identified as suspicious. Banks shall not tip off the customer regarding the filing of any suspicious transaction report. All KYC records must be made available to the authorities within forty eight hours of request.

SEED-CYBERSECURITY-section_5_multi_facto.raw_text: Banks must implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all administrative access to critical servers and customer-facing digital banking portals. MFA must include at least two independent factors: knowledge (password), possession (OTP/Token), or inherence (Biometrics) to protect customer sessions.

SEED-CYBERSECURITY-section_8_data_encryp.raw_text: All customer personal data and transaction data must be encrypted in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher protocols, and at rest using AES-256 or higher encryption algorithms. Encryption keys must be rotated at least once every 12 months to prevent unauthorized disclosure.

SEED-CYBERSECURITY-section_14_incident_r.raw_text: Any cyber security incident, security breach, or unauthorized access to bank databases must be reported to the RBI and CERT-In within 6 hours of detection or identification. A detailed forensic investigation report must be submitted within 14 days.

SEED-CYBERSECURITY-section_20_security_o.raw_text: Banks must operate a 24x7 Security Operations Center (SOC) to monitor, detect, and respond to potential threats in real-time. The SOC must perform automated log analysis of all critical infrastructure, firewall logs, and active directory activities.

SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_38_cust.section_title: Paragraph 38 - Customer Due Diligence for Individuals
SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_38_cust.raw_text: Banks shall carry out Customer Due Diligence by obtaining the following documents from individual customers at the time of account opening. A certified copy of any one document notified by the Central Government as proof of identity along with one document as proof of address is mandatory. Permanent Account Number or Form 60 as defined in Income Tax Rules shall be obtained compulsorily. Aadhaar number shall be obtained from customers who voluntarily provide it. No account shall be opened in anonymous or fictitious names. The identity verification must be completed within 24 hours of account opening initiation.

SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_45_peri.raw_text: Regulated entities shall ensure periodic updation of KYC records for all customers. The frequency of updation shall be at least once in two years for high risk customers, at least once in eight years for medium risk customers, and at least once in ten years for low risk customers. Banks shall obtain fresh KYC documents from customers when their risk profile changes from lower to higher category. Failure to complete periodic KYC updation shall result in partial freezing of the account whereby credits shall not be permitted. Only debit transactions essential for the livelihood of the customer shall be allowed after partial freeze. A notice period of thirty days shall be given to the customer before imposing partial freeze.

SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_56_vide.raw_text: The Video Customer Identification Process may be carried out by a bank official using live video. The process shall include a live photograph of the customer, capture of the PAN card, and an OTP sent to the customer's mobile number registered with Aadhaar. The bank official conducting the V-CIP must be a trained employee. All V-CIP sessions must be recorded and stored for a minimum period of six months. The video quality must be sufficient to identify the customer clearly. Customers whose Aadhaar is not seeded with a mobile number shall not be eligible for V-CIP.

SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_65_cent.raw_text: Banks shall register customers on the Central KYC Records Registry for all accounts opened on or after January 1, 2017. A unique fourteen digit KYC Identifier shall be issued to each customer registered on the CKYC Registry. Banks downloading KYC records of existing customers from the CKYC Registry shall carry out fresh physical verification of identity and address documents at the time of download. Physical re-verification of original documents is mandatory regardless of the availability of digital records in the Registry. No exemption from physical re-verification shall be granted to any category of customer including those with V-CIP history.

SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_72_enha.section_title: Paragraph 72 - Enhanced Due Diligence for High Risk Customers
SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_72_enha.raw_text: Banks shall apply enhanced due diligence measures for all customers classified as high risk. High risk customers include Politically Exposed Persons and their family members and close associates. Enhanced due diligence shall include obtaining source of funds documentation, senior management approval for account opening, and enhanced ongoing monitoring at quarterly intervals. Banks shall not open accounts for foreign nationals from high risk jurisdictions without prior approval from the Compliance department head. All high risk account opening decisions must be documented with specific reasons recorded.

SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_89_reco.section_title: Paragraph 89 - Record Keeping and Reporting Obligations
SEED-KYC_BASELINE_2024-paragraph_89_reco.raw_text: All records relating to the identity of customers and their transactions shall be maintained for a minimum period of five years after the business relationship has ended. Transaction records exceeding the prescribed threshold of ten lakh rupees shall be reported to the Financial Intelligence Unit within seven working days. Suspicious transaction reports shall be filed within seven working days of the transaction being identified as suspicious. Banks shall not tip off the customer regarding the filing of any suspicious transaction report. All KYC records must be made available to the authorities within forty eight hours of request.

SEED-DUMMY_CYBER_CIRCULAR_V1_2025-paragr.raw_text: The Board of Directors shall approve a cyber security policy annually and review its implementation at least once every quarter. Banks shall designate a Chief Information Security Officer who shall report directly to the Managing Director and shall not hold any concurrent operational technology role. The CISO shall present a consolidated cyber risk report to the Board's IT Strategy Committee at every meeting. Responsibility for cyber resilience shall rest with the Board and shall not be delegated to any external service provider.

Banks shall report all cyber security incidents to the Reserve Bank of India within six hours of detection. A detailed root cause analysis report must be submitted to the Department of Supervision within seven working days of the incident. Banks shall also notify CERT-In in accordance with the directions issued under Section 70B of the Information Technology Act, 2000. Incidents affecting customer data shall additionally be disclosed to affected customers within seventy two hours. Failure to report incidents within the prescribed timelines shall attract supervisory action.

Banks shall implement multi-factor authentication for all internet-facing applications used by customers and employees. For low-value transactions below five thousand rupees, banks may adopt risk-based authentication in place of a second factor. Administrative access to critical systems must be protected by hardware-based authentication tokens. Authentication logs shall be retained for a minimum period of one year.

SEED-DUMMY_CYBER_CIRCULAR_V1_2025-paragr.section_title: Paragraph 30 - Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing
SEED-DUMMY_CYBER_CIRCULAR_V1_2025-paragr.raw_text: Banks shall conduct vulnerability assessments of all critical information systems at least once every quarter. Penetration testing of internet-facing systems shall be carried out at least once every year by CERT-In empanelled auditors. All critical and high severity vulnerabilities must be remediated within fifteen days of identification. A summary of open vulnerabilities and remediation status shall be placed before the IT Strategy Committee every quarter.

Banks operating legacy systems that no longer receive security patches from the vendor shall isolate such systems in a segregated network zone. Compensating controls including enhanced monitoring and restricted access shall be documented for each legacy system. Banks shall prepare a decommissioning roadmap for all legacy systems and submit it to the Department of Supervision by March 31, 2026. No new business process shall be built on an unsupported legacy platform.

SEED-DUMMY_CYBER_CIRCULAR_V2_2026-paragr.raw_text: The Board of Directors shall approve a cyber security policy annually and review its implementation at least once every quarter. Banks shall designate a Chief Information Security Officer who shall report directly to the Managing Director and shall not hold any concurrent operational technology role. The CISO shall present a consolidated cyber risk report to the Board's IT Strategy Committee at every meeting. Responsibility for cyber resilience shall rest with the Board and shall not be delegated to any external service provider.

Banks shall report all cyber security incidents to the Reserve Bank of India within two hours of detection. A detailed root cause analysis report must be submitted to the Department of Supervision within five working days of the incident. Banks shall also notify CERT-In in accordance with the directions issued under Section 70B of the Information Technology Act, 2000. Incidents affecting customer data shall additionally be disclosed to affected customers within twenty four hours. Failure to report incidents within the prescribed timelines shall attract supervisory action.

Banks shall implement multi-factor authentication for all internet-facing applications used by customers and employees. Risk-based authentication shall not be used as a substitute for a second factor for any transaction irrespective of value. Administrative access to critical systems must be protected by hardware-based authentication tokens. Authentication logs shall be retained for a minimum period of two years.

SEED-DUMMY_CYBER_CIRCULAR_V2_2026-paragr.section_title: Paragraph 30 - Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing
SEED-DUMMY_CYBER_CIRCULAR_V2_2026-paragr.raw_text: Banks shall conduct vulnerability assessments of all critical information systems at least once every quarter. Penetration testing of internet-facing systems shall be carried out at least once every year by CERT-In empanelled auditors. All critical and high severity vulnerabilities must be remediated within fifteen days of identification. A summary of open vulnerabilities and remediation status shall be placed before the IT Strategy Committee every quarter.

Banks shall adopt a zero trust architecture for all critical information systems and are required to complete implementation within twelve months from the date of this circular. Every access request must be authenticated, authorized and encrypted regardless of network origin, and implicit trust based on network location is prohibited. Banks shall not permit any unmanaged device to connect to systems processing customer data. A board-approved zero trust implementation roadmap shall be submitted to the Department of Supervision within ninety days of the date of this circular. Non-compliance with these directions shall attract penal action under Section 47A of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.

(DIR Series) Circular No.17 June 24, 2026 To All Authorised Persons Madam / Sir, Modification of Returns / Reporting requirements under FEMA, 1999 Attention of Authorised Persons is invited to the provisions contained in the Foreign Exchange Management (Authorised Persons) Regulations, 2026, Master Direction – Money Changing Activities, Master Direction – Money Transfer Service Scheme (MTSS), and Master Direction – Reporting under Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999, as amended from time to time.

CIR-52909996bb0b::1.raw_text: On a review, it has been decided to rationalise certain reporting requirements, and prescribe/modify reporting formats pursuant to the Foreign Exchange Management (Authorised Persons) Regulations, 2026.

CIR-52909996bb0b::2.raw_text: The formats for submission of various returns are provided in the Annex to this circular. The following additional instructions shall apply: i) The revised format of FLM-8 shall also capture details relating to write-off of foreign currency notes. The requirement of obtaining prior approval of the Reserve Bank for write-off of foreign currency notes exceeding USD 2000 has been discontinued. Entities maintaining Nostro accounts and reporting the relevant transactions through FETERS shall not submit FLM-8 returns. ii) Authorised Persons having franchisee arrangements shall submit a list of such arrangements within 15 days from the end of each calendar quarter. iii) Indian Agents under MTSS shall submit the list of Sub-Agents on a quarterly basis within 15 days from the end of each calendar quarter.

CIR-52909996bb0b::3.raw_text: The following instructions / returns / forms stand discontinued: i) Prescribed formats of registers FLM-1 to FLM-7 under the Master Direction – Reporting under FEMA. However, FFMCs and non-bank AD Category-II entities shall continue to maintain complete and accurate records of all foreign exchange transactions undertaken by them and shall make such records available to the Reserve Bank for inspection/supervisory purposes, as and when required. ii) Return titled “Quarterly Statement showing summation of Foreign Currency Account opened in India out of export proceeds of Foreign Currency Notes / encashed Travellers’ Cheques.” iii) Requirement for submission of separate List of Additional Locations under MTSS and the requirement of quarterly confirmation regarding the veracity of the list published on the Reserve Bank’s website. iv) Return relating to Statement of Collateral under MTSS. Indian Agents shall, however, continue to ensure adequacy of collateral in terms of extant instructions.

CIR-52909996bb0b::4.raw_text: The directions contained in this circular have been issued under sections 10(4) and 11(1) of the Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 (42 of 1999) and are without prejudice to permissions / approvals, if any, required under any other law. Yours Sincerely, (N Senthil Kumar) Chief General Manager Annex FLM-8: Statement of purchases and sales of foreign currency notes [See paragraph 16(i) of Section V of Master Direction - Money Changing Activities] Name of the FFMC / Non-bank AD Cat-II: ____________________________ Licence No. ____________________ Month and Year of Return: ____________________________ USD GBP EURO JPY Others (Specify) A Opening Balance B Purchases: B.1 Purchases from Public B.2 Purchases through Franchisees / FxCs* B.3 Purchases from AD banks B.4 Purchases from other FFMCs / Non-bank ADs B.5 Imports C Sales: C.1 Sales to Public C.2 Sales through FxCs* C.3 Sales to AD banks C.4 Sales to other FFMCs / Non-bank ADs C.5 Exports D Foreign Currency Notes Written- off E Closing Balance * Not applicable to FFMCs Purpose‑code‑wise Purchases / Sales to Public during the month: Purpose Code Purpose Description Currency Amount List of Forex Correspondents (FxCs) appointed by Principal ADs [See Regulation 14(b) of Foreign Exchange Management (Authorised Persons) Regulations, 2026] Name of the Principal AD: ____________________________ Quarter ended: ____________________________ # Name of the FxC Address of FxC outlet # City State PIN Code Date of Appointment as FxC as per the agreement Foreign exchange facilities available at the FxC outlet 1 2 3 # In case an FxC operates through multiple outlets, the address of each outlet may be furnished in separate rows. List of Franchisees appointed by Authorised Persons [See paragraph 3(ii) of A.P. (DIR Series) Circular No.17 dated June 24, 2026] Name of the Authorised Person: ____________________________ Quarter ended: ____________________________ # Name of Franchisee Address of the Franchisee City State PIN Code Date of Appointment as Franchisee as per the agreement 1 2 3 Fit and Proper criteria [See Regulation 8(6)(c) of Foreign Exchange Management (Authorised Persons) Regulations, 2026] Name of the Entity: _____________________________________ Sr No. Particulars Information A. Personal details Full name Designation Present address Permanent address E-mail address Nationality

CIR-d9bcc6bbe90d::0.raw_text: In partial modification of the A.P. (DIR Series) Circular No. 13 dated June 8, 2026 on ‘NOP-INR position of Authorised Dealer Category-I banks’, it has been decided that AD Cat-I banks shall exclude the positions arising out of hedged transactions related to FCNR (B) deposits, External Commercial Borrowings and Overseas Foreign Currency Borrowings raised in terms of the aforesaid circulars, while ensuring compliance with the provisions of the A.P. (DIR Series) Circular No. 24 dated March 27, 2026 and for computation of net overnight open position in terms of the aforesaid Master Direction.

CIR-d9bcc6bbe90d::1.raw_text: The directions contained in this circular have been issued under Sections 10(4), 11(1) and 11(2) of the FEMA, 1999 (42 of 1999) and are without prejudice to permissions / approvals, if any, required under any other law. Yours faithfully, (Dimple Bhandia) Chief General Manager

human_review_queue.new_obligation: In partial modification of the A.P. (DIR Series) Circular No. 13 dated June 8, 2026 on ‘NOP-INR position of Authorised Dealer Category-I banks’, it has been decided that AD Cat-I banks shall exclude the positions arising out of hedged transactions related to FCNR (B) deposits, External Commercial Borrowings and Overseas Foreign Currency Borrowings raised in terms of the aforesaid circulars, while ensuring compliance with the provisions of the A.P. (DIR Series) Circular No. 24 dated March 27, 2026 and for computation of net overnight open position in terms of the aforesaid Master Direction.
